content.problemStatement: Write a Python program that reverse the given tuple and print its values.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the size of the tuple. The second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the tuple elements.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line with the elements of the tuple in reverse order, separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First tuple (1, 2) becomes (2, 1). Second tuple (3, 2, 1) becomes (1, 2, 3).

solution.approach: For each test case we read the length N and then read the N integers into an array (or list). Reversing is trivial: we output the stored elements starting from the last index down to the first. This requires O(N) time per test case and O(N) auxiliary space for the stored values (the input itself). No special data structures are needed.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the size of the tuple. Overall O(T·N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for storing the tuple elements.

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T; if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1) return 0; while(T--){int N; scanf("%d",&N); int a[1000]; for(int i=0;i<N;++i) scanf("%d",&a[i]); for(int i=N-1;i>=0;--i){printf("%d",a[i]); if(i) printf(" ");} printf("\n");} return 0;}
code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data=sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it=iter(data)
    T=int(next(it))
    out=[]
    for _ in range(T):
        N=int(next(it))
        arr=[next(it) for _ in range(N)]
        out.append(' '.join(reversed(arr)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__=='__main__':
    main()
